turns.content: Give me a translation and commentary. INCIPIT LIBER DE MYSTICA THEOLOGIA. CAPITULUM I. De mystica theologia. Trinitas superessentialis, et superdeus, et superoptime Christianorum inspector theosophiae, dirige nos in mysticorum eloquiorum superincognitum et superlucentem et sublimissimum verticem, ubi nova et absoluta et inconversibilia theologiae mysteria, secundum superlucentem absconduntur occulte docentis silentii caliginem, in obscurissimo, quod est supermanifestissimum, supersplendentem, et in qua omne relucet, et invisibilium superbonorum splendoribus superimplentem invisibiles intellectus. Mihi quidem haec opto. Tu autem, o amice Timothee, circa mysticas speculationes corroborato itinere et sensus desere, et intellectuales operationes, et sensibilia, et invisibilia, et omne non ens, et ens; et ad unitatem, ut possibile, inscius restituere ipsius, qui est super omnem essentiam et scientiam. Ea enim teipso et omnibus immensurabili et absoluto pure mentis excessu ad superessentialem divinarum tenebrarum radium, omnia deserens et ab omnibus absolutus ascendes. His autem, vide, quomodo nemo indoctorum auscultet. Indoctos autem dico, in his, quae sunt, conformatos, et nihil super existentia superessentialiter esse imaginantes. Sed his quidem hi, quos videre ea, quae secundum seipsos est, scientia oportet tenebras latibulum ejus. Si autem super hos sunt divinae in mysteria introductiones, quid quis dixerit de aliis ardentibus, quicunque omnibus superpositam causam ex ipsis in his, quae sunt, novissimis characterizant, et nihil eam superare aiunt ab ipsis fictarum impietatum et multiformium formationum? In ipsa etiam oportet omnes existentium ponere et affirmare positiones, veluti omnium causa, et omnes eas potentius negare, tanquam super omnia superexistente, et non aestimari depulsiones oppositas esse intentionibus, sed multo prius ipsam super privationes esse, quae est super omnem ablationem et positionem. Sic igitur divinus Bartholomaeus ait, et multam theologiam esse, et minimam, et evangelium latum, et magnum, et iterum correptum. Mihi videtur supernaturaliter intelligens, quia et multiloqua est optima omnium causa, et breviloqua simul, et sine verbo, quomodo neque verbum, neque intelligentiam habet, eo quod omnibus ipsa superessentialiter superposita est, et solis incircumvelate et vere manifesta, polluta omnia et immunda transgredientibus, et omnem omnium sanctarum summitatum ascensionem superascendentibus, et omnia divina lumina et sonos et verba caelestia superantibus, et in caliginem occidentibus, ubi vere est, ut eloquia aiunt, omnium summitas. Etenim non simpliciter divinus ipse Moyses primus mundari jubetur, et iterum ab his, qui tales non sunt, segregari, et post omnem purgationem audit multivocas tubas, et videt luminaria multa aperte fulgurantia, et multum fusos radios. Deinde multis segregatur, et cum electis sacerdotibus in summitatem divinarum ascensionum praecurrit: et si eis sic manentibus fit Deo, contemplatur vero non ipsum, invisibilis enim, sed locum ubi stetit. Hoc autem arbitror significare divinissima et sublimissima visibilium et intelligibilium, ypotheticos quosdam esse sermones, subjectorum omnia superanti, per quae super omnem intelligentiam ipsius praesentia ostenditur, intelligibilibus summitatibus sanctissimorum ejus locorum supergrediens: et quod ipsis absolvitur visibilibus et videntibus, et in caliginem ignorantiae occidit vere mysticam, per quam docet omnes gnosticas receptiones, in qua omne relucet, et invisibili innascitur omnis, qui est in omnium summitate, et a nullo, neque a seipso, neque altero, omnino autem ignoto omni scientia in otio per id quod melius est intellectus, et nihil cognoscendum super animum sic cognoscentium. CAPITULUM II. Quomodo oportet et uniri et hymnos referre omnium causali et super omnia. Juxta hanc nos fieri superlucentem oramus caliginem, et per invisibilitatem et ignorantiam videre et cognoscere ipsum super Deum et scientiam. Hoc non videre et scire, idipsum est vere videre et cognoscere, superessentialem superessentialiter laudare per omnium existentium ablationem, sicut per seipsum naturale agalma facientes, auferunt ea, quae superadjecta sunt, pura occulti visione vetantia, et ipsam in seipsa ablatione sola occultam manifestant formam. Oportet autem, ut arbitror, ablationes in contrarium positionibus laudare. Etenim illas quidem a praestantissimis inchoantes, et per media in novissima descendentes, apponimus. Hinc vero a novissimis ad principalissimas ascensiones facientes, omnia auferimus, ut incircumvelate cognoscamus illam ignorantiam, ab omnibus ignorantibus in omnibus existentibus circumvelatam, et superessentialem illam videamus caliginem, ab omni in existentibus luce occultatam. CAPITULUM III. Quae sunt καταφατικαί theologiae, quae αποφατικαί. In theologicis igitur characteribus potentissima affirmativae theologiae laudavimus, quomodo divina et optima natura unica dicitur, quomodo triadica, quae secundum ipsam dicta et paternitas, et filiolitas, quid vult declarare in spiritu theologia, quomodo ex immateriali et impartibili optimo in corde bonitatis germinata sunt lumina, et quomodo ipsius in ipso et in seipsis et inter se invicem coaeternae in germinatione mansionis servaverunt reditum, quomodo superessentialis Jesus humanis naturalibus veritatibus essentia factus est, et quaecunque alia ab eloquiis expressa sunt secundum theologicos characteres. In eo autem qui est de divinis nominibus, quomodo optimus nominatur, quomodo ων, quomodo vita, et sapientia, et virtus, et quaecunque alia intelligibilis sunt divinae nominationis. In symbolica vero theologia, quae sint a sensibilibus in divina transnominationes, quae divinae formae, quae divinae figurae, et partes, et organa, qui divini loci et mundi, qui furores, quae tristitiae et maniae, quae ebrietates et crapulae, quae juramenta, quae execrationes, qui somni, quae vigiliae, et quaecunque aliae symbolicae sunt divinae similitudinis sacre figuratae formationes. Et te arbitror considerasse, quomodo verbis copiosiora magis sunt novissima primis. Etenim habere theologicos characteres, et divinorum nominum reserationem breviorem verbis esse symbolica theologia. Quoniam quidem quantum ad superiora respicimus, tantum verba contemplationibus invisibilium coartantur: sicut et nunc in ipsam super intellectum occidentes caliginem, non brevem sermonem, sed sermonis defectum et nominationis inveniemus. Et ibi quidem desursum ad novissima descendens sermo, juxta quantitatem ejus, quae est universaliter ad proportionalem multitudinem, inventus est. Nunc autem ab his, quae deorsum sunt, ad superpositum ascendens, secundum mensuram invii corripitur, et post omne invium totus sine voce erit, et totus adunabitur sono carenti. Quare autem omnino, inquis, ex praestantissimo ponentes divinas positiones, a novissimis inchoamus divinam ablationem? Quia quid super omnem ponentes positionem, ex magis ipsi cognatiori conditionalem affirmationem oportuit ponere: quod autem super omnem ablationem auferentes, ex magis ipso distantibus auferre. An non magis est vita et bonitas, quam aer et lapis? Et magis non crapulae, et non maniae, quam non dicitur neque intelligitur? CAPITULUM IV. Quia nihil sensibilium omnis sensibilis per excellentiam causalis. Dicamus igitur sic: Omnium causa, et super omnia ens, neque carens essentia est, neque carens vita, neque irrationabilis est, neque insensualis, neque corpus est, neque figura, neque species, neque qualitatem, aut quantitatem, aut tumorem habet, neque in loco est, neque videtur, neque tactum sensibilem habet, neque sentitur, neque sensibilis est, neque inordinationem habet, neque perturbationem a passionibus materialibus commota, neque impotens est sensibilibus succumbens casibus, neque indigens est lucis, neque mutationem, aut corruptionem, aut partitionem, aut privationem, aut fluxum, neque aliud quid sensibilium est, neque habet CAPITULUM V. Quia nihil intelligibilium omnis intelligibilis per excellentiam causalis. Iterum autem ascendentes dicamus, ων neque anima est, neque intellectus, neque phantasiam, aut opinionem, aut verbum, aut intelligentiam habet, neque ratio est, neque intelligentia, neque dicitur, neque intelligitur, neque numerus est, neque ordo, neque magnitudo, neque parvitas, neque aequalitas, neque similitudo aut dissimilitudo, neque stat, neque movetur, neque silentium ducit, neque habet virtutem, neque virtus est, neque lux, neque vita est, neque hostia est, neque seculum, neque tempus, neque tactus est ejus intelligibilis, neque scientia, neque veritas est, neque regnum, neque sapientia, neque unum, neque unitas, neque deitas, aut bonitas, neque spiritus est, sicut nos scimus, neque filiolitas, neque paternitas, neque aliud quid nobis aut alicui existentium cognitum, neque quid non existentium, neque quid existentium est, neque existentia eam cognoscunt, an ipsa sit, neque ipsa cognoscit existentia, an existentia sunt, neque verbum ejus est, neque nomen, neque scientia, neque tenebrae est, neque lumen, neque error, neque veritas, neque est ejus universaliter positio, neque ablatio, sed eorum, quae post eam sunt, positiones et ablationes facientes, ipsam neque auferimus, neque ponimus, quoniam et super omnem positionem est perfecta et singularis omnium causa, super omnem ablationem excellentia omnium simpliciter perfectione, et summitas omnium. Explicit liber de mystica theologia.

Below is an English‐translation of the passage you provided—with one possible rendering of its notoriously dense and “baroque” mystical Latin—and a commentary explaining some of its themes and stylistic features. Note that the original text is highly complex, full of layered adjectives (especially the repeated “super‑” terms), and its syntax is intentionally obscure in order to evoke a transcendent mystery. There is no single “correct” translation, but the version below aims to convey both the literal sense and the flavor of its mystical language. Translation Incipit Liber de Mystica Theologia (Here begins the Book of Mystical Theology.) Chapter I. On Mystical Theology Translation: “O Trinity of the super‐essence, and super‑God, and the most excellent inspector of Christian theosophy, direct us toward that mystical peak of discourse which is beyond common knowledge—shining and most sublime—where new, absolute, and incommunicable mysteries of theology, in accordance with the radiant hidden (or ‘super‐radiant’) secret of the teaching silence’s darkness, are concealed. This darkness is the most obscure yet is also the most manifest and most resplendent, wherein all things shine, and wherein the invisible intellects are filled with the super‑splendors of invisible goodness. Such things I indeed desire. But you, O friend Timothee, having been strengthened by your journey and senses in mystical speculations, must abandon both intellectual operations and those things sensible and insensible, as well as both non‑being and being; and you must, as far as possible, restore that unity of which he is the source—the one who is above every essence and all knowledge. For by this very self and by all that is immeasurable and the absolutely pure excess of the mind, you abandon all things and ascend absolutely, separate from everything, to the super‑essential ray of divine darkness. And behold how none of the unlearned listens to these matters. To the unlearned I say that these things are conformed to that which exists, and that they imagine nothing beyond existence in a super‑essential manner. Yet regarding those who see that which is, their knowledge must contain the hiding-place of that darkness. But if there are divine introductions into the mysteries for these, then what shall one say of the others who burn with zeal—those who characterize everything with a super‑imposed cause, claiming that nothing can surpass that cause except the imputed myriad of false pieties and various forms? In that very thing it is necessary to establish and affirm the positions of all existing things—as if they were the cause of everything—and then to deny them even more powerfully, as though the super‑existing were above all, and not to regard the opposing pushes of removal as intentions but rather as pre‑existing privations which are above every removal and positioning. Thus, the divine Bartholomew says, there is much theology, and little theology, and the Gospel is broad, and great, and again corrected. It seems to me that he who understands supernaturally, for he is the best of all causes in many words yet brief at the same time and without utterance—just as he has neither word nor understanding—because all things are super‑imposed in a super‑essential way, and with only the circumambient and truly manifest, he overwhelms and defiles all that transgresses, and causes all the highest ascensions of the sanctified to surpass, and all the divine lights, sounds, and heavenly words to overcome, and in the setting darkness—where, truly, as they say, the highest of all is manifest. For it is not simply that the divine himself, as Moses, is commanded to be purified first, and again separated from those who are not such, and after all purification he hears many-voiced trumpets, and sees many lights shining forth openly, and many rays dispersed. Then he is separated into many parts, and he precedes with the elect priests into the summit of divine ascensions; and if in such a state they remain with God, then he is contemplated not as the invisible itself but as the place where he once stood. This, I judge, signifies that the most divine and highest of the visible and intelligible—the hypothetical words of certain subjects that overcome everything—through which the very presence of him is revealed beyond all understanding, surpassing even the intelligible summits of his most sanctified places—is what is expiated by the visible and the seers, and in the darkness of ignorance a true mysticism dies; by this mysticism all Gnostic receptions are taught, in which everything shines, and the invisible is born in everyone who is in the highest of all, and by none—not from itself, nor from another, but entirely unknown to all knowledge in repose by that which is the better part of the understanding—and nothing is to be known of the spirit by such knowing.” — End of Chapter I Chapter II. How All Things and Hymns Must Be United and Recounted as Causal and Over All Translation: “In accordance with this we pray that from the shining we be made, and through invisibility and ignorance we may see and know that which is above God and knowledge. Not to see and know this is to see and know truly—that is, to super‑essentially praise by the removal of all existing things—just as making for itself a natural image; it removes that which is super‑added by the pure, hidden vision that forbids, and in that very removal it alone reveals the hidden form. Yet it is necessary, I believe, to praise removals in contrary positions. For indeed we set forth those which begin with the most excellent, and descending from the middle into the very last; from these, making ascensions from the last to the principal, we remove everything, so that by the circumambient ignorance we may know that ignorance, hidden by all ignorant things in all that exists, and see that super‑essential darkness, hidden by every light in existence.” — End of Chapter II Chapter III. Concerning the “Καταφατικαί” (Affirmative) and “Αποφατικαί” (Negative) Theology Translation: “In our discussion of the theological characters of the most powerful affirmative theology, we have extolled how the divine and best nature is said to be unique, how it is triadic according to what is called Fatherhood and Filiality, and what the Spirit of theology intends to declare; how, from that which is immaterial and indivisible, in the best heart of goodness, lights are generated; and how in him, within himself and in himself and among one another, they have preserved the perpetual return in the dwelling of germination. We have also explained how the super‑essential Jesus became essence to human natural truths, and whatever else has been expressed by eloquence according to the theological characters. And in that which is concerning the divine names, we have shown how the best is named; how it is ὤν (being), how it is life, and wisdom, and virtue, and whatever else is intelligible in the divine designation. In what may be called symbolic theology—that is, that which comes from the sensible into divine trans‑nominations—we have treated those divine forms, those divine figures, and the parts and organs of the divine place and world; which passions, which sorrows and manias, which inebriations and debaucheries, which oaths, which execrations, which dreams, which vigils, and whatever else are the sacredly figurated formations of divine likeness. And I suppose you have considered how, with words, the more copious are newer than the earlier. For to have theological characters and the reservation of divine names in briefer words is what symbolic theology is. For as far as we look toward that which is higher, the words are constrained by the contemplations of the invisible: just as now, when falling into the super‑intelligible darkness, we find not a brief discourse but rather a defect of discourse and of designation. And indeed there, descending toward the very last, a speech is discovered according to its quantity, which is universal in proportion to the multitude; and now, ascending from that which is below to that which is super‑imposed, according to the measure of the invisible, it is corrected, and after all the invisible, all is without voice, and entirely united in a lack of sound. Therefore, I ask you, why should we not, having established the divine positions in the most excellent manner, begin with the divine removal from the last? For what is there but that one should establish a conditional affirmation known to itself as super‑imposed on every position—and then, removing everything above all removals, one removes from that which is farthest away. Is not life and goodness greater than air and stone? And are not inebriation and mania likewise not said nor understood?” — End of Chapter III Chapter IV. That Nothing of the Sensible Can Possess the Excellence of the Causal Translation: “Let us then say thus: The cause of all things, and above all beings, neither lacks essence nor life, is not irrational, nor insensible, nor is it a body, nor a figure, nor a species, nor does it have quality or quantity, nor any mass; it is neither in place nor appears, nor does it have any sensible touch, nor is it sensed, nor is it sensible; it has no ordering, nor is it disturbed by material passions, nor is it impotent before sensible events, nor does it lack light, nor undergo mutation, corruption, partition, privation, or flux, nor is there anything else of the sensible, nor does it have any.” — End of Chapter IV Chapter V. That Nothing of the Intelligible Can Possess the Excellence of the Causal Translation: “Again, ascending we say: That which is ὤν (being) has neither soul, nor intellect, nor imagination, nor opinion, nor word, nor understanding, nor reason; it has neither intelligence nor is it spoken of, nor is it understood, nor is it a number, nor an order, nor a magnitude, nor smallness, nor equality, nor likeness or unlikeness, nor is it static, nor does it move, nor does silence lead, nor does it have any power, nor is power itself; it is neither light nor life, nor a victim, nor an age, nor time, nor any sensible touch does its intelligible have, nor is it knowledge, nor truth, nor a kingdom, nor wisdom, nor one, nor unity, nor deity or goodness, nor is it spirit—as we know—nor filiality, nor paternity, nor anything else known to us or to any existing thing, nor of that which does not exist; nor is it that which exists, nor do existing things know it, or does it itself know existence, or are there things that exist in it; neither is its word nor name, nor is it knowledge, nor darkness, nor light, nor error, nor truth, nor is its universal position determined, nor is there removal—but rather those things that come after it, making positions and removals; for we neither remove nor establish it, since over every position it is perfect and singular as the cause of all things, over every removal it is the excellence of all in pure perfection, and the summit of all.” — End of Chapter V > Explicit: The Book of Mystical Theology is ended. It emphasizes that the ultimate divine reality is “beyond” both the categories of being and non‑being and beyond all forms of rational discourse. The Role of “Darkness” and “Light”: Ironically, the text describes a “divine darkness” that conceals the incommunicable mysteries, even as it is “the most manifest” and “resplendent” form. In mystical literature, darkness is often not a negation but a symbol of the unknowable, hidden nature of God—a darkness that is itself luminous when approached from a higher, inner vision. Affirmative vs. Negative Theology: In Chapter III, the discussion of “καταφατικαί” (affirmative) and “αποφατικαί” (negative) theology outlines two approaches: one that speaks of God by asserting what He is (using positive, symbolic images such as life, wisdom, and virtue) and one that negates any limited, finite description of the divine. Critique of the Unlearned: The writer warns that “none of the unlearned listens” to these mysteries, implying that such teachings are reserved for those who have undergone a certain spiritual or intellectual purification. Concluding Thoughts The final chapters (IV and V) sharply differentiate between the sensible and the intelligible realms, arguing that neither realm, with all its properties and limitations, can contain the causal excellence of the divine. In other words, God is not to be found within any particular attribute (like life, light, or truth) as these are merely relative and finite; rather, God is the ground of all being and non‑being, a mystery that defies all ordinary description. It employs an elaborate and highly technical vocabulary to instruct the reader in a method of negation—both of sensory and intellectual attributes—to ultimately arrive at a state of unity with the divine mystery. John Scotus Eriugena's Latin translation of Mystical Theology (Μυστική Θεολογία) by Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite is a fascinating case study in the transmission of Neoplatonic metaphysics into the Latin West. His rendering of the Greek text into Latin reflects both his deep engagement with Neoplatonism and his efforts to integrate Dionysian thought into a Carolingian intellectual framework. 1. Translation as Interpretation: Key Choices by Eriugena Eriugena was not merely translating words but transforming concepts into a Latin metaphysical idiom that was at times quite distinct from the Greek. a. "Superessentialis" for "ὑπερούσιος" (Hyperousios) Eriugena’s translation of ὑπερούσιος (hyperousios) as superessentialis is crucial. The Greek term means "beyond being" or "supra-ontological," a fundamental term in Dionysian and Proclean metaphysics. The super- prefix in Latin attempts to capture the Greek ὑπερ- ( hyper- ), denoting transcendence over being itself. However, while ὑπερούσιος retains a more active and processual sense in Greek, superessentialis risks implying a kind of static state of existence that is merely "above being" rather than dynamically "beyond it." This reflects Eriugena’s own metaphysical commitments: he saw divine reality as an ontological hierarchy, but one in which being itself could be understood in Neoplatonic terms as a dynamic unfolding ( processio ) and return ( reditus ), rather than as something entirely negated. b. "Superdeus" for "ὑπέρθεος" (Hypertheos) Eriugena's coinage of superdeus for ὑπέρθεος ( Hypertheos , "beyond God") is another fascinating translation choice. Greek ὑπέρθεος conveys a paradox: God is both "God" and "beyond the gods" in a way that destabilizes traditional theological categories. Superdeus retains this paradox but shifts it slightly toward a hierarchical framework in which "God" is explicitly seen as above other divine beings, rather than as beyond all divine predication. This aligns with Eriugena’s broader system in which God is both absolute transcendence and immediate immanence, a paradox he resolves in his Periphyseon by arguing that God is both nihil (nothing) and omnia (everything). c. "Caligo" for "Γνόφος" (Gnophos, "Darkness") Eriugena’s rendering of gnophos as caligo instead of tenebrae is a striking choice. In the Greek, gnophos (e.g., from Exodus 20:21, describing Moses entering "the divine darkness") is a technical term in apophatic theology. It represents not simply an absence of light but a kind of super-bright darkness, a paradoxical presence beyond human perception. Caligo in Latin, while retaining the sense of obscurity, can also suggest confusion or ignorance. This might be a potential shift in nuance—whereas Dionysius' gnophos signifies a divine unknowability filled with presence, caligo in Latin might suggest something more like the obscurity of fallen human knowledge. However, in later medieval mystical theology, particularly in Eckhart and the Rhineland mystics, caligo was taken up in precisely the Dionysian sense Eriugena was likely intending. 2. Eriugena’s Neoplatonism and the Structure of Reality Eriugena was deeply influenced by Proclus and the broader Neoplatonic tradition, and his translation choices reflect this. As Eric Perl notes, Dionysius' theology depends on the Neoplatonic triad of monē (remaining), proodos (procession), and epistrophē (return), in which all things emerge from the One and ultimately return to it【18:6†source】. Eriugena’s Latin, while often literal, tends to reinforce a metaphysical reading in which Dionysian apophasis (negation) is systematically aligned with Proclean return to the divine. This is particularly visible in how he handles Dionysius’ discussions of negation: His use of ablatio (removal) and negatio (negation) as translations of ἀφαίρεσις (aphaeresis) and ἀπόφασις (apophasis) emphasizes the structural logic of negative theology rather than its existential or experiential dimension. Eriugena often adds clarifications that make explicit a Proclean henological (unity-focused) metaphysics rather than a purely Dionysian theology of mystical ascent. 3. The Influence of Eriugena’s Translation Eriugena’s translation of Mystical Theology was not a neutral scholarly exercise; it shaped how Dionysius was received in the Latin Middle Ages. By providing Latin readers with Neoplatonic categories rendered in Latin scholastic terminology, he helped prepare the way for figures like Albertus Magnus, Thomas Aquinas, and Meister Eckhart to engage with Dionysian thought. However, because Eriugena’s translation subtly shifts the Dionysian vision toward a more systematic, hierarchical ontology, later medieval readers often interpreted Dionysius through a more rigid Neoplatonic framework than was perhaps present in the original Greek. 4. Conclusion: Eriugena as a Creative Translator Eriugena’s translation is not simply a passive Latinization but an active philosophical intervention. His choices of superessentialis , superdeus , and caligo reveal his deep commitment to integrating Dionysian thought into a framework that could be assimilated by Carolingian and later Scholastic thinkers. At the same time, his approach risks shifting the experiential, paradoxical, and mystical aspects of Dionysius into a more formalized and structured ontological system. This tension—between a Dionysian apophatic theology that resists conceptualization and an Eriugenian Neoplatonism that seeks to systematize it—would become a defining feature of later medieval thought on mysticism and negative theology. How does this excessus relate to the transcendental philosophy of Kant and Hegel

The concept of excessus in Eriugena’s mystical theology and Neoplatonism can be fruitfully compared to the transcendental philosophy of Kant and the dialectical idealism of Hegel. While these systems emerge from different historical and intellectual contexts, they share common concerns about the limits of human cognition, the dialectical structure of reality, and the role of negation in philosophical inquiry. 1. Eriugena’s Excessus and the Limits of Cognition Eriugena’s excessus describes the movement beyond sensory and rational knowledge toward an encounter with the divine through apophasis (negation). This corresponds to his broader ontology, in which God is both the source ( principium ) and the end ( reditus ) of all being. Excessus is not merely an epistemic limit but an ontological process: the human intellect moves beyond discursive knowledge into an unknowable darkness ( caligo ), where God is encountered beyond being ( superessentialis ). This movement is fundamentally dialectical : knowledge is attained through negation, culminating in a paradox where divine ignorance ( docta ignorantia ) becomes the highest form of knowing. This bears a resemblance to Kant’s transcendental limits and Hegel’s dialectical movement , but in different ways. 2. Kant: The Limits of Reason and the Noumenon Kant’s Critique of Pure Reason (1781) establishes a fundamental division between the phenomenal realm (what can be known through the categories of understanding) and the noumenal realm (what lies beyond possible experience). For Kant, reason has an inherent drive to exceed its own limits , leading to antinomies (self-contradictions). The noumenon (thing-in-itself) is an unknowable beyond, analogous in some ways to Eriugena’s superessentialis , which cannot be positively grasped. However, where Eriugena sees an ontological ascent into divine union, Kant maintains that this limit is absolute: we can think the noumenon, but we cannot know it. Comparison to Eriugena: Eriugena and Kant agree that human cognition has limits , but Kant denies the possibility of moving beyond them through mystical or dialectical ascent. In contrast, Eriugena sees excessus as an ontological imperative : the intellect is called to go beyond its own conditions toward union with the divine. 3. Hegel: Dialectics and the Overcoming of Finitude Hegel, in Phenomenology of Spirit (1807) and Science of Logic (1812-16), moves beyond Kant by resolving the problem of transcendence through dialectics . For Hegel, what appears as an absolute limit (like Kant’s noumenon) is actually a moment in the self-unfolding of Absolute Spirit. Negation as a Mode of Knowing: In Hegel’s dialectic, every finite concept negates itself and is sublated ( aufgehoben ) into a higher unity. The Unfolding of the Absolute: Hegel describes a logical movement from being to nothing to becoming, mirroring the way Eriugena describes God as beyond all categories ( nihil and omnia ). Human Thought Realizing Itself as Divine: Hegel’s Absolute Spirit ultimately recognizes itself through historical and philosophical development—an idea that echoes Eriugena’s notion that all being returns to God. Comparison to Eriugena: Eriugena and Hegel both use negation as a means of ascent , but Hegel’s negation is an immanent dialectical process, whereas Eriugena’s excessus is a mystical movement toward divine unknowability. Hegel internalizes transcendence : the Absolute is realized through history and philosophy, whereas Eriugena sees transcendence as an ontological movement beyond reason. 4. Conclusion: Excessus, Dialectics, and the Problem of Transcendence Feature Eriugena ( Excessus ) Kant ( Transcendental Limits ) Hegel ( Dialectics of Absolute Spirit ) Nature of the Beyond God as superessentialis , beyond being and knowing The noumenon is unknowable and unreachable The Absolute is realized through dialectical development Role of Negation Apophatic (negative) theology removes all predicates to reach divine unknowability Reason negates itself when it tries to exceed its limits Negation is dialectical: every finite concept is sublated into a higher unity Knowledge and Union The mind ascends beyond rationality into mystical union with the divine Human reason is limited to the phenomenal realm The dialectical process leads to Absolute Knowing Metaphysical Implications Reality is structured as a return to God ( reditus ) through excessus Knowledge is bounded by conditions of experience Spirit realizes itself through negation and self-overcoming Eriugena’s excessus anticipates Kant’s notion of the limits of cognition , but unlike Kant, he believes these limits can be transcended. Meanwhile, Hegel’s dialectics resonate with Eriugena’s negation , but where Eriugena negates to reach an unknowable God, Hegel negates to achieve self-knowledge. Thus, Eriugena’s thought can be seen as a pre-modern apophatic dialectic , pointing toward both Kantian epistemology and Hegelian metaphysics, but without collapsing divine transcendence into historical self-realization.

The terms are organized by chapter. Chapter I: De mystica theologia 1. Superessentialis (from Greek ὑπερούσιος ) Meaning: “Beyond essence” or “super-essential.” Metaphysical Significance: This term reflects the Neoplatonic idea that God is beyond being ( ἐπέκεινα τῆς οὐσίας in Plotinus). Eriugena’s use of superessentialis aligns with his ontology in Periphyseon , where God is both nihil (nothing) and omnia (everything). The divine reality is not just above being but constitutes the ineffable ground from which all being emerges. 2. Superdeus (from Greek ὑπέρθεος ) Meaning: “Beyond God.” Metaphysical Significance: A paradoxical term used to emphasize that the divine transcends all concepts of divinity. This aligns with Pseudo-Dionysius’ negative theology: even the name “God” must be negated to approach the unknowable divine reality. 3. Caligo (from Greek γνόφος ) Meaning: “Darkness” or “mystical obscurity.” Metaphysical Significance: The term conveys the Dionysian idea of the cloud of unknowing , where the highest divine knowledge is attained through ignorance. In Eriugena’s system, this represents the excessus of the mind beyond discursive reasoning into mystical union. 4. Ablatio (from Greek ἀφαίρεσις ) Meaning: “Removal” or “stripping away.” Metaphysical Significance: Essential to apophatic theology, ablatio is the process of negating finite attributes to reach divine transcendence. In Eriugena, this is also a cognitive process of purification, removing all determinations to attain pure divine presence. 5. Intellectus (from Greek νοῦς ) Meaning: “Intellect” or “higher mind.” Metaphysical Significance: Eriugena follows the Neoplatonic tradition in viewing intellectus as both human and divine. The intellectus must be stripped of its conceptual constraints through excessus (mystical transcendence) to reach divine truth. Chapter II: Quomodo oportet et uniri et hymnos referre 6. Mystica theologia (from Greek μυστικὴ θεολογία ) Meaning: “Mystical theology.” Metaphysical Significance: This term encapsulates the entire method of approaching the divine through negation and ineffability. Mystica in Latin retains its Greek meaning of hidden or initiatory knowledge, linking theology to direct, non-discursive experience. 7. Superlucens Meaning: “Super-shining” or “beyond light.” Metaphysical Significance: The paradox of divine darkness ( caligo ) being also superlucens reflects the Dionysian notion that God is both unknowable and the source of all illumination. In Eriugena’s thought, this connects to the idea that divine transcendence manifests through negation. 8. Silentium Meaning: “Silence.” Metaphysical Significance: Silence in Mystical Theology is not mere absence but the highest form of divine communication. Eriugena aligns with Dionysius in seeing silentium as a necessary stage of mystical ascent where speech fails. Chapter III: Quae sunt καταφατικαί theologiae, quae αποφατικαί 9. Cataphatica theologia (from Greek καταφατικὴ θεολογία ) Meaning: “Affirmative theology.” Metaphysical Significance: The method of describing God through positive attributes ( God is light, life, wisdom ). Eriugena acknowledges this approach but subordinates it to negative theology ( apophatica theologia ), which he considers higher. 10. Apophatica theologia (from Greek ἀποφατικὴ θεολογία ) Meaning: “Negative theology.” Metaphysical Significance: The negation of all concepts attributed to God. Eriugena extends Dionysian apophasis by incorporating it into his broader metaphysical system, in which all being emanates from God and returns through a process of negation and transcendence. 11. Nomina divina Meaning: “Divine names.” Metaphysical Significance: The divine names ( sapientia, vita, virtus ) are affirmations that are ultimately negated. Eriugena follows Dionysius in arguing that all names applied to God must be transcended to reach divine union. Chapter IV: Quia nihil sensibilium omnis sensibilis per excellentiam causalis 12. Nihil (from Greek μηδέν ) Meaning: “Nothingness.” Metaphysical Significance: Eriugena follows the Neoplatonic paradox where God is both nihil (because He transcends being) and omnia (because all things emerge from Him). This anticipates later medieval mysticism, especially Nicholas of Cusa’s docta ignorantia . 13. Sensibilia Meaning: “Sensible things.” Metaphysical Significance: Eriugena distinguishes sensibilia from intelligibilia (intelligible things), aligning with the Platonic hierarchy of reality where the sensible world is a shadow of divine truth. 14. Lux Meaning: “Light.” Metaphysical Significance: Divine light is both a metaphor for knowledge and something to be negated. In Dionysius and Eriugena, true enlightenment occurs through transcending light into the darkness ( caligo ). Chapter V: Quia nihil intelligibilium omnis intelligibilis per excellentiam causalis 15. Intelligibilia Meaning: “Intelligible things.” Metaphysical Significance: The realm of intelligibilia is higher than sensibilia but must still be negated in the mystical ascent. Eriugena incorporates this into his hierarchy of nature in Periphyseon , where intellect ( intellectus ) is still finite compared to God. 16. Unitas Meaning: “Unity.” Metaphysical Significance: The divine is the absolute unitas , beyond all multiplicity. Eriugena follows Proclus in seeing the One as prior to being, but he also integrates this into a Christian framework where unity and trinity coexist. 17. Excedere Meaning: “To transcend” ( excessus in noun form). Metaphysical Significance: The term describes the movement beyond being, knowledge, and sensation toward divine union. Excessus is not just a limit (as in Kant) but an active process leading to divinization ( deificatio ). 18. Docta ignorantia Meaning: “Learned ignorance.” Metaphysical Significance: Though not an explicit term in Eriugena’s translation, this idea pervades his work. It signifies that true knowledge of God comes through recognizing the limits of knowledge, anticipating Nicholas of Cusa’s later use of the term. Conclusion: The Metaphysical Function of Eriugena’s Latin Terms Eriugena’s translation choices are deeply metaphysical, reflecting both his fidelity to Dionysius and his own speculative theology. His use of: Superessentialis , nihil , and superdeus affirm his commitment to an apophatic vision of God. Caligo , silentium , and excessus reinforce the mystical ascent beyond rational thought. Ablatio , apophatica theologia , and unitas align with Neoplatonic structures of negation and return. In sum, Eriugena’s Latin terminology is both a faithful rendering of Dionysian negative theology and an expansion into a systematic Christian Neoplatonism that profoundly shaped medieval thought.

Tenebrae (from Greek σκότος , skotos ) These choices are not arbitrary but reflect subtle philosophical and theological distinctions in both Pseudo-Dionysius' apophatic theology and Eriugena's own metaphysical framework. 1. Caligo as Mystical Darkness Greek Source: γνόφος ( gnophos ) Eriugena’s Latin: Caligo Meaning: A divine, luminous darkness Metaphysical Significance Caligo conveys the paradoxical nature of the divine: it is unknowable yet overflowing with presence. In Dionysius , gnophos is used in reference to Moses ascending into the cloud of divine unknowing (Exodus 20:21). The idea is that God is beyond conceptual knowledge , so the closer one gets to Him, the less one "sees." Eriugena retains this meaning : divine knowledge is attained only through excessus , where the mind transcends itself into a radiant obscurity . 💡 Key Interpretation: Caligo is not just darkness in a literal sense, but an intellectual or mystical obscurity —a realm beyond being and knowing. 2. Tenebrae as Absence and Privation Greek Source: σκότος ( skotos ) Eriugena’s Latin: Tenebrae Meaning: Darkness as the absence of light Metaphysical Significance Tenebrae usually denotes privation —a darkness that results from the absence of divine illumination . In Dionysius , skotos often appears in contrast to phos (light), emphasizing the opposition between ignorance and knowledge . Eriugena uses tenebrae carefully : it often represents the lower, fallen darkness —that which lacks participation in divine light. 💡 Key Interpretation: Unlike caligo , which signifies the highest form of divine experience , tenebrae is negative —it represents separation from the source of light, akin to Plato’s Cave . 3. Why Does Eriugena Use Two Words for Darkness? Eriugena’s use of caligo and tenebrae reflects two different types of negation in Dionysian negative theology : Latin Term Greek Term Meaning Metaphysical Role Caligo Γνόφος ( gnophos ) Mystical darkness The paradoxical fullness of divine unknowability Tenebrae Σκότος ( skotos ) Darkness as absence Lack of divine illumination , akin to ignorance By distinguishing these two , Eriugena preserves the Neoplatonic hierarchy : Caligo belongs to theophany —a super-radiant obscurity where the divine is beyond comprehension. Tenebrae belongs to privation —a lower darkness where creatures are separated from divine light. This distinction is essential to Eriugena’s metaphysics, since he sees all reality as structured hierarchically : creatures move from tenebrae (ignorance) toward caligo (mystical darkness), where they can be reunited with the divine. 4. Influence on Later Mysticism This dual concept of darkness profoundly influenced later medieval mystics, including: Meister Eckhart : Who speaks of the "Divine Darkness" ( caligo as the highest knowledge). Nicholas of Cusa : His docta ignorantia builds on Eriugena’s idea that God is known in the unknown . The Cloud of Unknowing : Which describes mystical ascent as moving beyond reason into divine obscurity. 5. Conclusion: Darkness as the Path to God Eriugena’s careful distinction between caligo (the luminous obscurity of God) and tenebrae (the absence of divine presence) is a brilliant philosophical adaptation of Dionysius’ Greek into Latin. This reinforces his broader system where: God is unknowable yet present ( caligo ). Creatures are in darkness until they return ( tenebrae → caligo → divine union). Thus, for Eriugena, the journey to God is not toward light, but into the highest form of darkness —a mystical negation where knowledge ends and union begins.

Expanded Glossary of Latin Terms in Eriugena’s Translation of Mystical Theology (Focusing on key Neoplatonic concepts without repeating previously covered terms) This expanded glossary highlights Neoplatonic metaphysical concepts in Eriugena’s translation, showing how he adapts Dionysius’ thought into a Latin philosophical framework . The terms emphasize hierarchical being, procession and return, divine unity, and apophatic negation. 1. Hierarchical Being and the Divine Order 36. Principium (from Greek ἀρχή , archē ) Meaning: “Principle” or “origin.” Neoplatonic Significance: Archē in Neoplatonism refers to the ultimate source of reality —the One ( τὸ ἕν ) or God as the first principle. Eriugena’s use of principium reflects his belief that God is the origin of all things but also beyond origin . In Plotinus , archē is the One, from which all things emanate ( proodos ). In Dionysius , archē is the hidden source —God is beyond all predicates yet the cause of all. 37. Causalis (from Greek αἴτιος , aitios ) Meaning: “Causal” or “pertaining to cause.” Neoplatonic Significance: In Neoplatonism , the One is the universal cause of all reality. Dionysius calls God the causa omnium (“cause of all”), which Eriugena translates to emphasize God’s transcendental causality . Unlike Aristotelian causality , this divine causality is non-dual : God is not just a first mover but a source that overflows into creation . 38. Processio (from Greek πρόοδος , proodos ) Meaning: “Procession” or “emanation.” Neoplatonic Significance: Processio describes how all things “flow out” from the divine without diminishing it. Plotinus’ triadic structure : 1. Monē (remaining in the One) 2. Proodos (procession from the One) 3. Epistrophē (return to the One) Dionysius and Eriugena adopt this: creation is a processio from God and a reditus (return) to Him. Christianized Neoplatonism : Procession is not necessary emanation , but divine self-giving ( theophany ). 39. Reditus (from Greek ἐπιστροφή , epistrophē ) Meaning: “Return.” Neoplatonic Significance: In Plotinus , epistrophē is the soul’s journey back to the One after descending into multiplicity. Eriugena’s theology is structured around reditus : - All things originate in God ( principium ). - All things proceed from God ( processio ). - All things return to God ( reditus ). Mystical union is the highest form of reditus . 2. Unity and Multiplicity 40. Unitas (from Greek ἕν , hen ) Meaning: “Oneness” or “unity.” Neoplatonic Significance: The One (τὸ ἕν) in Plotinus is beyond being , utterly simple , and the source of all reality . Dionysius and Eriugena preserve this absolute unity as the foundation of negative theology : - God is not “one” in a numerical sense (because that would limit Him). - God is beyond unity and plurality . - The intellect can only approach unity by negating multiplicity . 41. Multiplicitas (from Greek πολλαπλότης , pollaplótēs ) Meaning: “Multiplicity” or “manifoldness.” Neoplatonic Significance: In Neoplatonism, the One overflows into multiplicity through processio . Eriugena translates Dionysius’ paradox : - God is neither one nor many . - The divine unitas manifests as multiplicitas without division. Mystical ascent : Moving from the many back to the One is the soul’s goal. 3. Apophatic and Cataphatic Theology 42. Negatio (from Greek ἀπόφασις , apophasis ) Meaning: “Negation.” Neoplatonic Significance: In Dionysius , negation is the only way to describe God ( the via negativa ). Eriugena systematizes this : every divine predicate must be: 1. Affirmed ( cataphasis – "God is light") 2. Denied ( apophasis – "God is not light") 3. Super-negated ("God is beyond light"). 43. Affirmatio (from Greek κατάφασις , kataphasis ) Meaning: “Affirmation.” Neoplatonic Significance: Affirmation is the lower form of theology, where divine names ( bonitas, vita, lux ) are provisional truths . Eriugena’s innovation : Affirmations must always be negated , since God is beyond being. 44. Transcendere (from Greek ὑπερβαίνειν , hyperbainein ) Meaning: “To transcend.” Neoplatonic Significance: In Dionysius , transcendence means going beyond all concepts ( excessus ). Eriugena’s translation strengthens this : - God transcends all things ( superessentialis ). - The soul transcends knowledge into mystical ignorance ( docta ignorantia ). 45. Ineffabilis (from Greek ἄρρητος , arrhētos ) Meaning: “Inexpressible.” Neoplatonic Significance: Eriugena’s key concept : God is beyond speech, and the highest theology is silence ( silentium ). Mystical paradox : - The more one knows, the less one can say . - The highest name for God is no name . 4. Theophany and Divine Manifestation 46. Theophania (from Greek θεοφάνεια , theophaneia ) Meaning: “Manifestation of God.” Neoplatonic Significance: In Dionysius , all things manifest traces of the divine . Eriugena expands this : - Creation is itself a theophany . - The material world participates in divine being . 47. Participatio (from Greek μέθεξις , methexis ) Meaning: “Participation.” Neoplatonic Significance: Being is not self-sufficient —it participates in the divine. Eriugena’s adaptation : - The soul participates in God through knowledge. - All beings “borrow” their existence from the divine. Conclusion: Eriugena’s Neoplatonic Vision Eriugena’s translation preserves and transforms Neoplatonic thought. His Latin emphasizes: 1. Procession and Return ( processio → reditus ). 2. Divine Transcendence ( unitas , negatio ). 3. Mystical Ascent ( excessus , silentium ). 4. Participation ( theophania , participatio ). Thus, his translation is not just linguistic—it is a philosophical statement , shaping medieval Christian Neoplatonism.

By linking Biblical revelation with Neoplatonic metaphysics , Eriugena reinterprets Scripture through Dionysian apophaticism , shaping medieval mystical theology .

Yes, that was the end of the text ( Explicit Liber de Mystica Theologia ), so now I will address your question on how the view of unity in this text compares to the One in pagan Neoplatonism and Proclus' theology . Comparison of Dionysian Apophaticism with Pagan Neoplatonism and Proclus The Dionysian view of unity as presented in The Mystical Theology (and reflected in Eriugena’s Latin translation) is radically apophatic —even the term “One” is inadequate to describe God. This distinguishes Dionysius from pagan Neoplatonists , particularly Plotinus and Proclus , who, while also embracing apophaticism , still regard "The One" (τό ἕν) as the best available term for the ineffable divine principle. 1. Dionysius and the Inadequacy of “Unity” In The Mystical Theology , Dionysius goes beyond even Neoplatonic negation: God is not One ( neque unum, neque unitas ). God is not Being ( neque existentia ). God is not even Non-Being ( neque quid non existentium ). God is beyond affirmation and negation ( neque positio, neque ablatio ). This means that even the concept of "unity" is too restrictive —because unity still implies relation (a unity of something) and still allows for conceptual grasping . 📌 Key Difference from Neoplatonism: For Plotinus and Proclus , the One is ineffable and beyond being, but it is still called the "One" because it is the principle of all things . Dionysius, however, rejects even this —since calling God "One" still imposes a conceptual limit . 2. The One in Plotinus (Enneads) In Plotinus’ system ( Enneads ), the One : Is beyond Being ( ἐπέκεινα τῆς οὐσίας ). Is absolutely simple ( ἁπλότης ). Overflows (ἔκχυσις) into Intellect (Nous) and Soul. Can be approached through intellectual ascent and mystical contemplation . 📌 Similarity to Dionysius: Plotinus also insists that the One is unknowable . The ascent to the One involves a kind of “unknowing” (similar to Dionysius' apophatic ascent). Union with the One is mystical and beyond rational comprehension . 📌 Key Difference: The One still has a structure of emanation (One → Intellect → Soul → World). Dionysius rejects emanationism in favor of a more biblical, transcendent God . The One is still called "One" in Plotinus, whereas Dionysius refuses even that term. 3. Proclus and the Henads In Proclus’ system ( Elements of Theology ), the One is: The first principle , beyond being. The source of all reality through hierarchical emanation . Multiplied through Henads —divine aspects that preserve unity but allow for differentiation . 📌 Similarity to Dionysius: Both emphasize radical transcendence . Both use extreme negation to speak about the divine . Both accept hierarchical structures in reality (Dionysius’ angelic orders mirror Proclus' metaphysical hierarchy). 📌 Key Difference: Proclus still maintains a structured, hierarchical descent from the One (One → Henads → Intellect → Soul → Matter). Dionysius rejects any necessity of emanation —God is not “flowing out” but wholly beyond . For Proclus, the One is still named —for Dionysius, even the One must be negated . 4. Theological Implications 📌 Dionysius vs. Pagan Neoplatonism Feature Plotinus & Proclus (Pagan Neoplatonism) Dionysius (Christian Neoplatonism) First Principle The One ( τὸ ἕν ) Beyond One ( neque unum, neque unitas ) Being Beyond Being but source of all Beyond Being but wholly transcendent Emanation Hierarchical emanation ( πρόοδος ) No necessary emanation Return Mystical return through contemplation Mystical ascent through negation Language Still names God as "One" Denies even this —absolute apophaticism Relation to World The One necessarily overflows God is wholly free in creation 📌 Key Takeaway: Dionysius takes the logic of Neoplatonic negation further than even Plotinus or Proclus. Neoplatonism still holds that the One is the principle of being —Dionysius denies even that. For Proclus, the Henads allow for divine participation —Dionysius leaves only pure unknowability . 5. Conclusion: Apophaticism Beyond Neoplatonism Dionysius and Eriugena push beyond Proclus and Plotinus , rejecting even the most refined Neoplatonic terms : 1. God is beyond unity ( neque unum, neque unitas ). 2. God is beyond even the logic of the One . 3. The ascent is not just intellectual, but a radical unknowing ( divine darkness ). 4. No necessary emanation—God is wholly free and beyond participation . Thus, while pagan Neoplatonists still name the ultimate reality as "One" , Dionysius refuses even that name —God is not One, not many, not even "beyond being" in any way that can be comprehended . This is the purest apophatic theology possible.

The Language of Participation in Plato and Aristotle The language of participation ( methexis / κοινωνία ) is primarily developed by Plato , critiqued by Aristotle , and later systematized by Neoplatonists . Below are specific citations from Plato and Aristotle where the concept appears. 📖 Plato’s Language of Participation Plato uses methexis (μέθεξις, “participation”) and koinonia (κοινωνία, “communion”) to describe how particulars relate to Forms . 1. Methexis (Participation) - Plato, Parmenides 130e-131a > “Each of the forms is one, but because they participate in it, each of the many things is what it is.” 🔹 Relevance : Forms exist independently , but particulars partake ( metechousin ) in them. 🔹 Dionysius : The structure of participation influences Christian mystical hierarchy —all things reflect God’s divine essence but do not exhaust it . 2. Koinonia (Communion/Participation) - Plato, Sophist 251c > “Being, motion, and rest commune ( koinonei ) with each other.” 🔹 Relevance : Dionysius’ Divine Names ( Onomata Theou ) recall Plato’s notion of universal principles participating in the highest reality . 3. Hierarchical Participation - Plato, Timaeus 27d-28a > “Everything that comes to be must derive from some cause. The demiurge models the world on eternal Forms.” 🔹 Relevance : Timaeus presents a hierarchical chain of being , where participation links all things to the divine —mirroring Dionysius’ angelic orders ( Celestial Hierarchy ). 📖 Aristotle’s Critique of Participation Aristotle rejects Plato’s participation model , arguing that Forms cannot logically explain the connection between particulars and universals . 1. Rejection of Separate Forms - Aristotle, Metaphysics I, 990b > “To say that things are what they are by participating ( metechon ) in the Forms is to speak poetically, not scientifically.” 🔹 Relevance : Dionysius preserves a form of participation , but makes it unknowable ( neque participatio, neque separatio est ). 2. No Third Realm of Being - Aristotle, Metaphysics XIII, 1086b > “If the One exists separately, things cannot participate in it without contradiction.” 🔹 Relevance : This anticipates Proclus’ introduction of Henads , which mediate between the One and particulars —a structure echoed in Dionysius’ angelic hierarchy . 📖 Post-Platonic Philosophers on Participation After Aristotle, Middle Platonists and Neoplatonists reinterpret participation to bridge the gap between the One and the many . Alexander of Aphrodisias (fl. 200 AD) A commentator on Aristotle, Alexander reintroduces participation language in a more Neoplatonic direction. 1. Participation as Mediation - Alexander, On the Soul 88.10-20 > “Intellect is both participated in and participates in the divine.” 🔹 Relevance : This anticipates later Neoplatonism , where Intellect is both recipient and source of divine emanation —like Dionysius’ mystical ascent ( mentis excessu ). 2. Participation and Causality - Alexander, Commentary on Aristotle's Metaphysics 110.5 > “Things receive their being through participation in a higher principle.” 🔹 Relevance : This is more metaphysically structured than Aristotle and prepares the way for Proclus’ doctrine of mediated participation . 📖 Middle Platonists (1st Century BC - 2nd Century AD) Middle Platonists like Alcinous, Numenius, and Plutarch expand participation into religious and mystical contexts . Alcinous ( Didaskalikos , 9.5-10) - Alcinous, The Handbook of Platonism 9.5 > “The First Principle is beyond being, and all things partake ( metechousi ) of it through Nous.” 🔹 Relevance : This directly prefigures Dionysius , where Intellect (Nous) is an intermediary between God and creation . Numenius (frag. 11, 17, 27) - Numenius, On the Good 11 > “The supreme Good is the One. Soul must participate ( metexein ) in it to be purified.” 🔹 Relevance : Numenius' hierarchical participation structure influences Dionysius’ mysticism . Plutarch ( On the E at Delphi , 393c) - Plutarch, On the E at Delphi 393c > “Being is not univocal but participated ( metexomenon ).” 🔹 Relevance : Suggests that participation is the key to divine understanding , much like Dionysius’ angelic ascent. 📖 Neoplatonists (3rd - 6th Century AD) The most systematic developments of participation come from Plotinus and Proclus .
